item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice pun! A literal block that prevents people from being able to write! +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this a decent amount; the actual contents of the book were definitely what sold it for me. Reminds me of a lot of the lines from Getting Over It with Bennett Foddy, another exercise of futility narrated by a cynical and condescending author that's out mock the predicament that it itself set upon you.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very neat idea, amusing and horrifying at the same time, and carried well to its conclusion. I'm not totally certain some of the notes in the test log are needed (especially the last one about reprimanding Teals) but that's only a minor concern.

I also fixed a mistake in the second paragraph: I assumed that instances of the block appeared to prevent writing, not the book.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First change is relatively simple, 38 changed to 44.

There were actually two attempts, but it's easy to see what you mean. I'll add probably just one more on that part. I'm not sure how to amend your lack of interest with the other tests though.

The book is supposed to feel like a waste of time, but I guess that got the point across entirely too well. Essentially, the transcript is there to show the sadistic nature of the contents without being overly fluffy and to hit an emotional vein, but guess I missed the mark for you. Fixed the quote box.

me trying to change your view of the transcript through story
– hide block

You are a civilian. For the past half week, you have been unable to document anything. Your job, your ideas, your methods of communication have all been in the trash for four days now. The mystical blocky curse from the heavens have stopped you from doing anything. You can't call the police or a witch or do anything to help alleviate your current situation. You can't explain anything that's happening. This goes against all you know about the traditional world. You feel irrational and angry constantly. But in the corner of your mind, you remember seeing the blocks before. Perhaps in a bookstore, or on a book your friend owned? Randomly, a group of government people burst through your door and force you to read a book that will supposedly cure this mystical ailment. This book, however, is several hundreds of pages long. You are only a reader of moderate speed. A book like this will take upwards of 8 hours worth of your time, and they want you to read it in one sitting. You settle down with a bunch of armed strangers in your home, and begin to read. The book starts off strong by mocking you for being a moron. For the next 976 pages, you are mocked and screwed with on a basic mental level. You lose faith and interest in anything and everything. Against your better judgement, you want to attack someone and/or destroy the book. You don't trust the government people; you don't trust the author; hell, you don't even trust yourself at this point. You reach the end and suddenly can write again. Exhausted and confused, you turn to question one of the government people. He proceeds to grab you and spray something in your face. And you wake up. You check your phone. Your friends and your work highly concerned for you, but you can't remember having caused any alarm. When you think about it though, you can't remember the past five days. What is going on? But there aren't any answers for you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wonderful. Your hatred fuels me like oil does a lamp. I'm glad that people are getting both the comedic and the subtle essence of pure evil horror from the article. I personally love big books, but being mocked for several hours would probably be enough to break me. Just imagine being the first reader though. The author is mindfucking you for HUNDREDS of pages. You don't know for sure that the thing will help you. It may be completely useless for all you know. Just a terrible, horrifying thought.

Thanks for the comment and +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the comprehensive review!

Addressing said review:

+ show block
– hide block
  • I think I'll do that.
  • Glad that bit didn't come off as too contrived. Will make changes for more clinical tone to address the second part.
  • I'll add some more detail to that.
  • No, the profanity isn't actually a cognitohazard, but if you were an angry researcher trying to cover his words and actions (which in this case, were truly extreme and inappropriate), wouldn't you make something to that effect up? While highly unethical, I would. I'll think about removing the censoring, but I'd like to think that it's the Foundation equivalent of "I don't believe you, but I also don't care about calling you out." Your final comment there makes me want to add that. I think if I add that the prior cognitohazard was created by Teals, working your idea in will be a cinch. Edit: At the moment, have decided against this action. Can't pin down why specifically though. Not feeling it enough? Apathy it is.
  • I was wondering if that wasn't clear enough. Basically, it has no self preservation kind of thing going for it. Multiple stimuli, including audible and physical, were "tested" upon the object, it didn't react to those stimuli. Florence was also retroactively justifying the reaction of Teals a bit. Glad the main point of comedy came off well, but do you recommend thta I change this to be clearer?
  • Will change.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a really spectacular read. The whole thing just made me so frustrated.

The idea is simple, the characters are colorful, and it's not too long. I enjoyed this SCP a lot. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As others have said, this isn't bad for a first SCP. There are some possible areas for improvement, but It's a cool idea and it all hangs together pretty well.

+1

A few comments:

+ show block
– hide block

The author, R. Sebastian, has authored several other anomalies; attempts to track and capture this person are ongoing.

Minor note, but a some other articles would provide a person of interest number, like "(designated PoI-####)", for the author. It's not really necessary, more of a personal/style choice, just mentioning.

Sustained interaction with SCP-3039-1 has proven deleterious to mental health, causing intense anger and poor decision making in subjects. This is theorized to be an additional effect of SCP-3039-1, and not human reaction to the anomaly, due to the extreme shifts in the typical composure of several test subjects.

You've disarmed me a bit here. Usually I tell authors that having people massively and angrily overreact to something is an unrealistic portrayal of human behavior, and that SCPs that mentally influence everyone to act in a specific way are poor writing. Those things are both present, but I like the balance here. I do feel for Teals, which is what both of those criticisms are meant to improve.

Intense anger and poor decision-making aren't exactly technical terms from mental health, though.

Experiment 15: Procedure: Subject exposed to a cognitohazard attempting to counter SCP-3039.

This is a pretty vague. Like, dI'd they create one to just nullify it, or were they trying to alter behavior? It's just a little odd to rely so much on them being able to build cognitohazards without just a smidge more detail.

Procedure: Subject opens SCP-3039’s storage locker and inflicts significant damage to several instances with an axe while screaming counter cognitohazard, “[EXPLETIVE] you, you [EXPLETIVE], blocky [EXPLETIVE]. Let me [EXPLETIVE] write, you [EXPLETIVE]!”

So… The Foundation has a cognitohazard that is just angry swearing? Also, you don't have to remove expletives. If you were redacting them because knowing the exact expletives and order constitutes a cognitohazard, that's funny enough to warrant a footnote.

Reprimand aside, this incident shows the anomaly has no degree of sentience as previously theorized, and that the effect is only provoked under certain circumstances.

I don't understand how it shows this, or how yelling a cognitohazard at it was going to cause anything, even if it was sentient. It was funny, though.

Not really, as it not even a physical object, but I like to imagine it does.

Typo here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
God, I don't know which is worse — the poison or the antidote. I mean, with my lifestyle I couldn't afford to not read it, but given how poorly I do with long books, I can imagine having several breakdowns if I tried to read THAT.

This is so deliciously sadistic, and it hits on a personal fear (insecurity?) of mine. I hate you. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The note prior to test 38 seems out-of-place, as both state that the personnel is going to be temporarily removed from duty.

Beyond that, the tests weren't nearly as interesting as they could have been - and it suffers from some lost potential with some 'telling' in the place of 'showing' here:

While his constant attempts to circumvent SCP-3039-1 are commendable, and his current progress is far from nominal

We only actually get to see one such attempt. Everything prior to that point was merely establishing how the item works (which is what the description is for). While it makes sense in-universe to have tests like that, it's not always conductive to an interesting narrative.

Finally, the entries from the book weren't amusing enough to merit half a dozen excerpts, and feels, ironically, like a waste of time. By the by, the final quote box seems to encompass further documentation from the SCPF, but it's formatted as if it's another book excerpt,

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd like to note something. One of the largest problems I have with some of the newly created SCPs is the lack of any consistent theme. To me, they just read like a random idea that's been surrounded by a large amount of text. This one actually has an interesting idea and isn't too long. 9/10 for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reasons
– hide block
  • First and main reason was because of plot convenience. It would have been less entertaining (and shorter) had they realized the truth immediately.
  • Second reason is because of unnecessary risk. The Foundation has 22 personnel afflicted. Due to the decent supply of test subjects, why waste a D-class that could be better used elsewhere? And these people are trained, possibly even highly trained. Why put their lives into the equation without taking other, less risky routes first? The researcher got tired of the blocks and of the pussy-footing of the Foundation; he pointed out this possiblity of no risk and volunteered to put his life at risk.
  • Third reason is because previous experience. The entity who wrote this is known for making these anomalous novels and releasing them to the mainstream. This indicates that it has semi-malicious intent in mind. Though the primary hazard is on the cover, the very releasing of the book appears actively harmful. It is reasonable to assume that there would be more to it than simply the cover.

But basically, it boils down to plot convinience. If you have ideas for writing the article to be more logically sound, feel free to suggest them. I'd be happy to make the change if it improves the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A like this, but I don't know why they thought the book was filled with infohazards in the first place? They just assumed it was and didn't test that at all until the researcher had been banging his head against it for however long?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, and will do. Also, you stole my first post, you monster.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My first SCP. Hopefully, my attempt to turn my writer's block into an effective counter to further blockage proves successful.

Huge thanks to bianhook55 for all the comprehensive viewing, suggestions, and fixing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. It's a creative idea. You might want to put in another collapsible for the experiements, and edit the main list so you can have a name instead of "[ACCESS DENIED]"

+1
